output_summary: aligned schemas for datasets: dbpedia, metacritic, sales; generated 2 schema correspondence groups.
profiled datasets: dbpedia, metacritic, sales.
Reused the blocking configuration from state.
Blocking uses `semantic_similarity` for dbpedia_sales, metacritic_dbpedia, metacritic_sales, blocking on name, platform, releaseYear.
Performance-wise, pair completeness ranges from 0.91 to 1.00 and candidate counts range from 409504 to 929573.
The config also records dataset id columns.

matcher mode `rule_based` with config source reused from state; evaluated pairs: id_columns, matching_strategies; selected scores: matching_strategies (threshold=0.75, f1=0.864367816091954, threshold=0.8, f1=0.8866995073891625, threshold=0.78); produced 2 pair strategy entries.
This run was an initial draft of the pipeline.
The pipeline loads three CSV datasets named dbpedia, metacritic, and sales from the schema-matching output directory.

The pipeline adds three pairwise EmbeddingBlocker configurations for dbpedia-sales, metacritic-dbpedia, and metacritic-sales using the sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 model with sklearn indexing and top_k set to 20.
The pipeline adds three rule-based matching configurations with dataset-pair-specific comparator sets, weights, and thresholds for dbpedia-sales, metacritic-dbpedia, and metacritic-sales.
The pipeline writes the three correspondence outputs to output/correspondences as separate CSV files and then concatenates them for fusion.
The pipeline adds a DataFusionStrategy with attribute fusers for 10 fields and runs a DataFusionEngine to produce output/data_fusion/fusion_data.csv.
The run adopts embedding-based blocking for candidate generation and rule-based weighted matching for pairwise entity resolution.
The matching behavior is customized by dataset pair instead of using one shared comparator recipe across all sources.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 1; fusion_data.csv was produced.

matching_config artifact updated from cluster analysis of correspondences_metacritic_dbpedia.csv; diagnosis flagged many multiple close-scoring matches and ambiguous duplicates; recommended_action=update_matching_config, recommended_strategy=None; health_metrics: total_clusters=4430, max_cluster_size=1076, small_cluster_ratio=0.4422, large_cluster_ratio=0.2104, one_to_many_ratio=0.6206, ambiguous_ratio=0.4712, max_degree=20, is_healthy=false, meets_target=false.
This run was an initial draft refinement of the pipeline rather than a repair.
The rule-based matcher weights were retuned for all three pairwise matching tasks while keeping the same blockers, comparators, thresholds, and fusion strategy.
The dbpedia-sales weights changed from [0.45, 0.2, 0.15, 0.2] to [0.36, 0.19, 0.11, 0.34].
The metacritic-dbpedia weights changed from [0.45, 0.2, 0.2, 0.15] to [0.42, 0.23, 0.08, 0.27].
The lowercase preprocessing helper was rewritten from a lambda expression to a named function without changing its behavior.
Matching behavior shifted toward giving more influence to releaseYear in all three pairwise matchers.
The dbpedia-sales matcher reduced emphasis on name, platform, and developer while substantially increasing the releaseYear contribution.
The metacritic-dbpedia matcher reduced developer weight and increased both platform and releaseYear influence.
The metacritic-sales matcher reduced name, platform, and developer emphasis while increasing criticScore and releaseYear influence, with userScore unchanged.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 2; fusion_data.csv was produced.

pipeline.py changed `lower_strip` from lambda to named function `def lower_strip(x): return str(x).lower().strip()`; matcher weights changed for dbpedia↔sales `[0.45,0.2,0.15,0.2]→[0.36,0.19,0.11,0.34]`, metacritic↔dbpedia `[0.45,0.2,0.2,0.15]→[0.42,0.23,0.08,0.27]`, metacritic↔sales `[0.35,0.2,0.15,0.12,0.08,0.1]→[0.28,0.16,0.08,0.2,0.08,0.2]`; matching_config artifact updated; cluster summary written with run id.
This run was an initial draft of the pipeline adaptation.
The matching weights were retuned for all three pairwise matchers while keeping the blocking setup, comparators, thresholds, and fusion strategy unchanged.
For dbpedia-sales, the weights changed from [0.36, 0.19, 0.11, 0.34] to [0.5, 0.24, 0.16, 0.1].
For metacritic-dbpedia, the weights changed from [0.42, 0.23, 0.08, 0.27] to [0.5, 0.27, 0.13, 0.1].
For metacritic-sales, the weights changed from [0.28, 0.16, 0.08, 0.2, 0.08, 0.2] to [0.2, 0.18, 0.12, 0.22, 0.18, 0.1].
The matching behavior shifted toward giving more influence to string and score comparators and less influence to releaseYear in all pairwise matchers.
In dbpedia-sales and metacritic-dbpedia, name and platform received higher weight while the date comparator weight was reduced to 0.1.
In metacritic-sales, criticScore and userScore gained more weight, developer and platform increased modestly, name decreased, and releaseYear was reduced to 0.1.
The embedding blockers, text columns, top_k, model, batch size, and id column were unchanged across all dataset pairs.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 3; fusion_data.csv was produced.

pipeline.py matching weights changed in 3 RuleBasedMatcher calls: dbpedia↔sales [0.36,0.19,0.11,0.34]→[0.5,0.24,0.16,0.1]; metacritic↔dbpedia [0.42,0.23,0.08,0.27]→[0.5,0.27,0.13,0.1]; metacritic↔sales [0.28,0.16,0.08,0.2,0.08,0.2]→[0.2,0.18,0.12,0.22,0.18,0.1]; matching_config artifact updated; cluster summary/result/history written for this run_id after analysis flagged correspondences_metacritic_dbpedia unhealthy with recommended_action=update_matching_config.
This run was an initial draft that created a new evaluation script for the fused output.
The dominant strategy was to rebuild evaluation around gold-id-aligned fused rows and then assign field-specific comparison functions by data type and semantics.
Name, developer, platform, series, publisher, and ESRB were evaluated with tokenized_match.
Release year was evaluated with year_only_match.
CriticScore, userScore, and globalSales were evaluated with numeric_tolerance_match.
Detected list-like columns, excluding internal identifier and metadata fields, were normalized and then evaluated with set_equality_match.
The run added build_eval_view to map fused rows to gold ids using parsed _fusion_sources, detected gold-id prefixes, and fallback to cluster _id when needed.
The run added _parse_source_ids to read source-id collections from native iterables or serialized JSON and Python literals.
The run added _detect_gold_prefix to infer the dominant gold id prefix from the gold dataset.
The run sorted aligned fused rows by numeric _fusion_confidence and dropped duplicate eval_id values to keep the highest-confidence candidate.
The run added list-like column detection and normalization across fused and gold data before evaluation.
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
overall accuracy is 72.58%; evaluation attempt counter is 1.
The run is failing mainly because noisy matched clusters are being fused transitively, causing false-positive cluster fusion and identity swaps across different games.
The current fusion logic is too naive for contaminated clusters and amplifies matcher ambiguity instead of resisting it.
A secondary problem is fusion rule bias, where longest_string and maximum systematically select verbose or inflated values rather than correct ones.
The evaluation metrics are very poor, with overall_accuracy 0.72581058823529413, macro_accuracy 0.175, 21 correct out of 119 evaluations, and only 15 records evaluated.

Identity-related accuracies are extremely low, with name_accuracy 0.13333333333333333, platform_accuracy 0.06666666666666667, releaseYear_accuracy 0.06666666666666667, and developer_accuracy 0.06666666666666667.
Numeric fields are completely wrong, with criticScore_accuracy 0.0 and userScore_accuracy 0.0, which is consistent with wrong records being fused together and with using maximum as the score fuser.
The analysis reports wholesale identity swaps in debug mismatches, including an expected Army of Two: The Devil's Cartel record being fused as Deadly Premonition: The Director's Cut.
This run was an initial draft of the pipeline adaptation.
The pipeline added a new data fusion stage that concatenates all three correspondence tables and runs a single DataFusionEngine over dbpedia, metacritic, and sales records.
The fusion strategy assigned longest_string to name, releaseYear, developer, platform, series, publisher, and ESRB.
The fusion strategy assigned maximum to criticScore, userScore, and globalSales.

The pipeline persisted the pairwise correspondence outputs to output/correspondences and wrote fusion debug output to output/data_fusion/debug_fusion_data.jsonl and fused results to output/data_fusion/fusion_data.csv.
The run shifted behavior toward raw connected-component fusion over all pairwise correspondences without additional pruning or subclustering.
The run relied primarily on verbosity-based selection through longest_string for identity and categorical attributes rather than consensus or source-priority selection.
The run used extremum-based numeric fusion through maximum for score fields, which biases fused values upward.
The run excluded singleton records by setting include_singletons to False.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 1; fusion_data.csv was produced.

cluster_analysis produced cluster_analysis_result/history and wrote cluster_summary_path with cluster_summary_run_id; analysis on correspondences_metacritic_dbpedia.csv flagged recommended_action=update_matching_config with diagnosis of many close-scoring matches/ambiguous duplicates; health_metrics total_clusters=4191,max_cluster_size=1268,small_cluster_ratio=0.4603,large_cluster_ratio=0.2145,one_to_many_ratio=0.6418,ambiguous_ratio=0.5327,meets_target=false,is_healthy=false.
This run was an evaluation refinement rather than an initial draft or a repair.

The dominant strategy was to adapt evaluation around noisy fusion outputs by emphasizing robust, attribute-aware assessment and by interpreting the current failures as wrong-cluster fusion rather than blocking failure.
The analysis prioritized future evaluation and fusion changes that resist transitive cluster contamination, favor consensus over extremes, and use source-priority and canonicalization.

Identity and descriptive string fields such as name, platform, developer, publisher, and ESRB were grouped as fields harmed by longest-string bias and in need of canonicalization plus majority-vote or source-priority handling.
Temporal and numeric fields such as releaseYear, criticScore, and userScore were grouped as fields needing parsed-year or median-style handling instead of longest_string or maximum.
List-like fields were treated as a separate family through automatic detection and normalization, with unregistered list columns assigned set_equality_match during evaluation.
The evaluation script retained helper logic for parsing source ids, detecting the gold-id prefix, and expanding fused rows into an evaluation view keyed by eval_id.
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
overall accuracy is 78.45%; evaluation attempt counter is 2.
The run is failing mainly because fusion logic is producing false positives from over-merged or ambiguous clusters.
The current fusion rules amplify noisy correspondences by selecting extreme or arbitrary values instead of consensus values.
Overall accuracy is 0.78457058823529413, with macro accuracy 0.175 across 119 evaluations and only 21 correct results.

The worst attribute accuracies are criticScore at 0.0, userScore at 0.0, releaseYear at 0.06666666666666667, platform at 0.06666666666666667, developer at 0.06666666666666667, and name at 0.13333333333333333.
Blocking completeness appears strong rather than deficient, with reported pair completeness of 0.913 for dbpedia-sales, 0.963 for metacritic-dbpedia, and 1.0 for metacritic-sales.

Cluster diagnostics indicate unhealthy correspondence graphs with huge maximum cluster sizes of 1097 and 1268, high one-to-many ratios, and hubs driven by common developer, platform, and releaseYear values.
The current pipeline fuses releaseYear with longest_string, criticScore with maximum, userScore with maximum, and ESRB with longest_string.
This run was an initial draft of the pipeline.
The pipeline added a first-pass fusion strategy that assigned longest_string to name, releaseYear, developer, platform, series, publisher, and ESRB.
The pipeline assigned maximum to criticScore, userScore, and globalSales in the fusion stage.
The run introduced full blocking and rule-based matching configuration for dbpedia-sales, metacritic-dbpedia, and metacritic-sales, and then concatenated all correspondence outputs before fusion.
The fusion behavior in this run relied primarily on generic attribute-wise selection rules rather than consensus-aware or source-aware fusion.

The matching setup stayed upstream and fixed, while the practical behavior of the run shifted toward trusting any record that entered a cluster and then selecting longest strings or maxima from that cluster.
The dominant fusion behavior favored verbose string variants for text-like fields and extreme values for numeric score fields.
The draft added thresholds of 0.75 for dbpedia-sales, 0.8 for metacritic-dbpedia, and 0.78 for metacritic-sales.
The run used embedding-based blockers and rule-based matchers with string, numeric, and date comparators across the three dataset pairs.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 1; fusion_data.csv was produced.

cluster_analysis produced new analysis/result history and wrote cluster summary artifact/run metadata (`cluster_summary_path`, `cluster_summary_run_id`, `cluster_summary_written`); `matching_config` artifact was updated and `matching_config_updated=true`.
This run was a refinement in evaluation mode rather than an initial draft or repair.

The dominant strategy was to refine evaluation alignment and reporting while keeping field-specific evaluators centered on tokenized text matching, year-only date matching, and numeric tolerance matching.
The run also shifted generic list-like field evaluation from set equality matching to tokenized matching.
Name, developer, publisher, platform, and ESRB were grouped under tokenized_match evaluation.
ReleaseYear was evaluated separately with year_only_match.
CriticScore and userScore were grouped under numeric_tolerance_match.
Detected list-like columns not already assigned explicit evaluators were grouped under tokenized_match in the updated script instead of set_equality_match.
The evaluation script added CSV loading via load_csv while retaining XML loading for the gold set.
The script preserved and reused eval_id expansion from _fusion_sources to align fused rows with gold IDs.
The import fallback search for list_normalization.py was simplified to direct __file__ parent paths rather than conditional globals checks.
The run replaced the main() wrapper with top-level execution flow.
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
overall accuracy is 78.65%; evaluation attempt counter is 3.
Run results were persisted for reproducibility and offline analysis.
